label: Privacy by Design and Default
description: Protection is incorporated into architecture, product decisions, operational processes, and default settings rather than added only after deployment.
summary: Privacy by Design is an engineering and governance discipline: foreseeable privacy risks are addressed throughout the system lifecycle, while default configurations limit exposure without requiring an individual to take protective action.
aliases: data protection by design and by default

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPT Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Article 25 — Data protection by design and by default

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Data Minimization / Minimum Necessary Collection
description: Collection, use, access, and retention are limited to data that is relevant and reasonably necessary for a defined purpose.
summary: Data minimization asks whether each data element, recipient, use, and retention period is needed for the stated objective. The analysis is contextual and should be revisited when the purpose, system, or risk profile changes.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPT Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Article 5 — Principles relating to processing of personal data

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Purpose Limitation and Secondary Use
description: Data is collected for specified purposes and later use is assessed for compatibility, authorization, and the expectations created at collection.
summary: Purpose limitation connects the reason for collection to later processing. A new use may require a compatibility analysis, a fresh legal basis or permission, additional notice, safeguards, or a decision not to proceed.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPP/E Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Article 5 — Principles relating to processing of personal data

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Lawfulness, Consent and Choice
description: Processing requires a valid legal or institutional justification, with consent and user choice evaluated as distinct mechanisms rather than universal defaults.
summary: Lawfulness analysis identifies the authority for processing and its conditions. Consent is only one possible basis and must be evaluated for validity, withdrawal, power imbalance, and whether another rule permits or restricts the activity.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPP/E Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Article 6 — Lawfulness of processing

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Automated Decision Safeguards
description: Limits, procedural protections, explanations, contestability, or human involvement for consequential automated decisions.
summary: Automated-decision safeguards address how consequential outputs are explained, reviewed, challenged, corrected, or overridden. Their scope depends on the type of decision, level of automation, legal effect, and governing instrument.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge
GDPR Article 22 — Automated individual decision-making, including profiling

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Individual and Data Subject Rights
description: Access, correction, deletion, objection, portability, restriction, complaint, and related mechanisms through which people can exercise agency over data practices.
summary: Individual-rights regimes differ in who may exercise a right, what data or processing it covers, verification rules, exceptions, response periods, and remedies. A shared label therefore does not imply equivalent scope.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPP/E Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Chapter III — Rights of the data subject

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Transparency, Notice and Explainability
description: Information that makes an AI or data practice visible, understandable, and open to scrutiny by affected people and oversight bodies.
summary: Transparency covers layered duties: notice about data practices, disclosure that AI is involved, documentation for reviewers, and explanations suitable for a particular audience and decision. These duties are related but not interchangeable.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge
EU AI Act official publication

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Fairness and Non-discrimination
description: Assessment and mitigation of unjustified differences in treatment, outcomes, access, or burden across people and groups.
summary: Fairness is context-dependent and can involve legal non-discrimination rules, representative data, performance across groups, allocation choices, accessibility, and procedural opportunities to question an outcome.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge
OECD AI Principles

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Sensitive Data and Vulnerable People
description: Heightened safeguards for data, contexts, or populations associated with greater risk of harm, exploitation, exclusion, or loss of autonomy.
summary: Sensitive-data rules vary by jurisdiction and may focus on enumerated categories, children, biometric or health data, inferred traits, vulnerable situations, or the consequences of processing rather than the label alone.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPP/E Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Article 9 — Processing of special categories of personal data

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Data Quality, Provenance and Training-data Governance
description: Requirements or practices governing the provenance, quality, legality, representativeness, documentation, and stewardship of data used to develop or evaluate AI.
summary: Training-data governance follows where data came from, whether it may be used, how it was prepared, whose interests may be underrepresented, what limitations are known, and how changes can be traced across model versions.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge
EU AI Act Article 10 — Data and data governance

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Retention, Deletion and the Data Lifecycle
description: Rules and controls governing how long data and derived artifacts are kept, reviewed, archived, deleted, or rendered no longer identifiable.
summary: Lifecycle governance links retention schedules to purpose, legal holds, security, model and backup dependencies, deletion feasibility, and evidence of disposal. Keeping data indefinitely is a design choice, not a neutral default.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPM Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Article 5 — Principles relating to processing of personal data

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Cross-border Data Governance
description: Conditions, safeguards, restrictions, and accountability mechanisms governing access to or transfer of personal data across borders.
summary: Cross-border governance requires more than locating a server. It may turn on the exporter, importer, remote access, destination, adequacy findings, contractual or statutory mechanisms, localization rules, and continuing safeguards.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPP/E Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Chapter V — Transfers of personal data to third countries or international organisations

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: AI Risk Management
description: Lifecycle identification, assessment, prioritization, treatment, documentation, and monitoring of risks arising from AI systems and their use contexts.
summary: AI risk management links technical performance to people, organizational objectives, use context, foreseeable misuse, and legal obligations. It is iterative because models, deployments, dependencies, and observed impacts change.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge
NIST AI Risk Management Framework

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Risk and Impact Assessment
description: A documented assessment conducted before or during a higher-risk data or AI activity to identify impacts, affected groups, mitigations, and residual risk.
summary: Impact assessments create a structured record of purpose, necessity, stakeholders, risk scenarios, safeguards, consultation, decisions, and review triggers. DPIAs, PIAs and AI impact assessments are related forms with different legal scopes.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPM Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Article 35 — Data protection impact assessment

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Accountability, Roles and Documentation
description: Assigned responsibility, policies, records, reporting lines, decision rights, and organizational structures for trustworthy data and AI practices.
summary: Accountability turns principles into owned work: roles are assigned, decisions and exceptions are documented, resources and escalation paths exist, controls can be tested, and leadership can explain how obligations are met.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPM Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Article 5 — Accountability principle

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Third-party and Supply-chain Governance
description: Due diligence, contracting, allocation of responsibility, access controls, monitoring, and exit planning for vendors and other external dependencies.
summary: Third-party governance follows risk across processors, model and data providers, cloud services, deployers, distributors, and downstream users. Contract terms support but do not replace oversight, evidence, and clear responsibility.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPM Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Continuous Monitoring, Audit and Metrics
description: Ongoing testing, monitoring, audit, metrics, issue tracking, and review used to determine whether controls and assumptions remain effective.
summary: Continuous assurance replaces one-time approval with observable performance. Useful measures connect known risks and obligations to thresholds, owners, evidence, remediation, reassessment, and escalation when conditions change.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPM Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Security, Resilience and Least Privilege
description: Technical and organizational measures used to protect data, systems, models, and services against unauthorized access, alteration, loss, misuse, and disruption.
summary: Security safeguards are selected according to context and risk, then maintained across identity, access, infrastructure, software, data, models, personnel, and recovery. Confidentiality is one objective alongside integrity, availability, and resilience.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPT Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Article 32 — Security of processing

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Privacy-enhancing Technologies and De-identification
description: Technical methods that reduce identifiability, limit disclosure, distribute trust, or permit useful processing with less exposure of underlying data.
summary: Privacy-enhancing technologies include context-specific approaches such as pseudonymization, aggregation, differential privacy, federated methods, secure computation, and encryption. No technique eliminates governance or re-identification analysis by itself.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPT Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
NIST Privacy Framework

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Incident Response, Notification and Redress
description: Preparation, detection, containment, remediation, documentation, notification, recovery, and redress for security, privacy, or AI incidents.
summary: Incident response coordinates technical containment with evidence preservation, legal assessment, communications, notification thresholds, affected-person support, recovery, and lessons learned. Deadlines and reportable events vary across regimes.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP CIPM Body of Knowledge and Exam Blueprint
GDPR Article 33 — Notification of a personal data breach

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Human Oversight
description: Meaningful human involvement, review, intervention, escalation, or accountability in the design, deployment, and operation of automated systems.
summary: Human oversight is effective only when people have appropriate authority, information, competence, time, and interface support to detect problems and intervene. A nominal approval step may not provide meaningful control.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge
EU AI Act Article 14 — Human oversight

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Frontier-model Safety
description: Evaluation, reporting, safeguards, security, and incident practices associated with highly capable or advanced general-purpose AI models.
summary: Frontier-model safety examines capability and risk evaluations, misuse pathways, systemic effects, model security, deployment controls, incident reporting, and the limits of current measurement for rapidly advancing systems.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge
Bletchley Declaration, 2023

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.

label: Global AI Coordination
description: International cooperation, shared science, policy dialogue, standards coordination, interoperability, and capacity building for AI governance.
summary: Global coordination seeks shared understanding and practical cooperation without assuming that institutions, standards, declarations, or national laws have the same authority. Governance remains plural and sensitive to local context.

sourceBasis.label: IAPP AIGP Body of Knowledge
OECD AI Principles

editorial.note: Project-authored learning summary informed by the cited public materials; it is not an IAPP definition, endorsement, or legal conclusion.
